notable.notable_blurb: Medal of Honor general; eldest Roosevelt son

narrative_blocks.header: Only general in D-Day's first wave
narrative_blocks.body: At fifty-six, with a heart condition and walking with a cane, Roosevelt was the only general to land with the first wave at Utah Beach on June 6, 1944. He died of a heart attack five weeks later and received the Medal of Honor posthumously.

narrative_blocks.header: He turned a landing error into a foothold
narrative_blocks.body: When the first craft drifted a mile south of their mark, he walked the fire-swept beach, met each arriving regiment, and improvised the attack from where they actually stood. Bradley later called it the bravest thing he saw in the war.

narrative_blocks.header: A father-son Medal of Honor, a century apart
narrative_blocks.body: His posthumous Medal of Honor and his father's, awarded at last in 2001 for San Juan Hill, made the Roosevelts only the second father and son both to earn it. The other pair were the two MacArthurs.

narrative_blocks.header: Father and son both hit the beaches
narrative_blocks.body: He was the only father to land with a son on D-Day; his boy Quentin came ashore at Omaha while he led at Utah. The family's soldiering instinct had carried into a second world war.

narrative_blocks.header: Dead of a heart attack five weeks later
narrative_blocks.body: He never saw the medal coming; a heart attack killed him in Normandy about five weeks after the landing. He was buried near the front and later moved to lie beside his brother Quentin, lost flying in the first war.

bio.bio_blurb: Medal of Honor general; eldest Roosevelt son

military_service.awards: Distinguished Service Cross; French Croix de Guerre
military_service.notes: Wounded at Soissons and gassed at Cantigny.

military_service.awards: Medal of Honor (posthumous, presented to his widow September 1944)
military_service.notes: Operation Torch (North Africa), Tunisia, and Sicily before leading the first wave ashore at Utah Beach on D-Day; died of a heart attack at Meautis, France, July 12, 1944.

notable.notable_blurb: Medal of Honor general; eldest Roosevelt son

bio.bio_blurb: Medal of Honor general; eldest Roosevelt son

military_service.awards: Distinguished Service Cross; French Croix de Guerre
military_service.notes: Wounded at Soissons and gassed at Cantigny.

military_service.awards: Medal of Honor (posthumous, presented to his widow September 1944)
military_service.notes: Operation Torch (North Africa), Tunisia, and Sicily before leading the first wave ashore at Utah Beach on D-Day; died of a heart attack at Meautis, France, July 12, 1944.

narrative_blocks.header: Red Cross and YMCA work across two wars
narrative_blocks.body: Eleanor ran YMCA canteens in France in the First World War and directed the American Red Cross Club in England in the Second. She organized the first American women's committee for China relief in 1937.

notable.notable_blurb: Photographer; first grandchild of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: Roosevelt's first grandchild, behind the camera
narrative_blocks.body: Grace was Theodore Roosevelt's first grandchild and trained at the Sorbonne as a freelance photographer. She gave the Library of Congress thousands of her own photographs alongside her mother's albums.

narrative_blocks.header: She gave 5,000 photographs to the nation
narrative_blocks.body: Over a long career she photographed presidents and visiting dignitaries up close. In 1986 she handed the Library of Congress some five thousand of her images, together with her mother's albums.

cross_connections.display_label: the fashion model who turned photographer

bio.bio_blurb: Photographer; first grandchild of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: He saved a Johns Hopkins landmark dome
narrative_blocks.body: A Princeton-trained Baltimore architect, McMillan designed country houses and the chapel at Garrison Forest School. He won the rescue of the old Johns Hopkins Hospital dome from demolition and its place on the National Register.

narrative_blocks.header: She rescued books from the Florence flood
narrative_blocks.body: The Smithsonian's first paper conservator, McMillan was sent to Florence in 1969 to help save books and prints drowned by the Arno's catastrophic flood. She rose to chief conservator and helped found the Washington Conservation Guild.

narrative_blocks.header: She gave her blue Mustang to the Smithsonian
narrative_blocks.body: McMillan donated her iconic powder-blue 1965 Ford Mustang to the Smithsonian in 2004. Legend held she was once ticketed for riding the express lanes with an inflatable man propped in the passenger seat.

notable.notable_blurb: Banker and Navy aviator; Roosevelt grandson

narrative_blocks.header: Banker who flew for the wartime Navy
narrative_blocks.body: After working at DuPont and serving as a Navy aviator in the Pacific, Roosevelt became a partner in the Philadelphia brokerage Montgomery, Scott. He also served as Pennsylvania's Secretary of Commerce.

bio.bio_blurb: Banker and Navy aviator; Roosevelt grandson

military_service.notes: Naval aviator and flag lieutenant in the Pacific theater; Air Medal.

notable.notable_blurb: CIA technical-services chief and art collector

narrative_blocks.header: A Roosevelt who ran the CIA's gadget shop
narrative_blocks.body: Cornelius headed the CIA's Technical Services Division, the branch that built espionage devices and guarded against electronic eavesdropping. A mining engineer and Navy veteran, he gave the National Gallery a major collection of Escher prints.

narrative_blocks.header: The spymaster who collected Escher
narrative_blocks.body: The same mind that ran the CIA's technical shop built one of the great private hoards of M. C. Escher prints, alongside Japanese netsuke and Andean finds. He later gave more than fifty of the Escher works to the National Gallery of Art.

cross_connections.display_label: a Roosevelt cousin who ran the 1953 Iran coup
the Revolution's spymaster, Washington's Culper Ring chief

military_service.notes: Served in the Navy and Naval Reserve, 1942-1951.

notable.notable_blurb: Soldier and aviation executive; Roosevelt grandson

narrative_blocks.header: His plane went down off Hong Kong
narrative_blocks.body: Named for his uncle the fallen WWI aviator, Quentin landed at Omaha Beach on D-Day while his father landed at Utah. He became a China aviation executive and died at twenty-nine in a Hong Kong plane crash, an end echoing his namesake's.

narrative_blocks.header: At fourteen he found a new fossil species
narrative_blocks.body: As a boy Quentin explored an Arizona cave and unearthed an extinct pronghorn, described as a new species in a museum paper. At nineteen he traveled alone into western China and brought back a thousand rare Naxi manuscripts for the Library of Congress.

narrative_blocks.header: His Harvard thesis on a vanishing script
narrative_blocks.body: As a student he traveled to the Tibetan borderlands of western China and brought back rare Naxi manuscripts, whose pictographs faintly recall Egyptian hieroglyphs. He made them the subject of his Harvard senior thesis before the Army claimed him.

cross_connections.display_label: the WWI aviator uncle he was named for

bio.bio_blurb: Soldier and aviation executive; Roosevelt grandson

military_service.notes: Artillery officer; wounded at Kasserine Pass; landed in the first wave at Omaha Beach. Silver Star, Purple Heart, Croix de Guerre.

narrative_blocks.header: She sketched the Alger Hiss trial
narrative_blocks.body: A Red Cross worker who married Quentin Roosevelt two months before D-Day, Frances became a portrait artist at Oyster Bay. She also worked as a courtroom sketch artist, covering the perjury trial of Alger Hiss.

narrative_blocks.header: She doubled the known age of Amazon settlement
narrative_blocks.body: Excavating a painted cave on the Amazon, Roosevelt found human habitation far older than scholars believed and some of the oldest pottery in the Americas. Her work overturned the notion that the rainforest could not sustain complex ancient societies.

cross_connections.display_label: a Berkeley-trained doctor who summited Mt Whitney with Muir

notable.notable_blurb: Sinologist, lawyer, and First Lady of Massachusetts

narrative_blocks.header: China-law scholar and Massachusetts First Lady
narrative_blocks.body: A Harvard-trained scholar of ancient Chinese law, Weld served as general counsel to a congressional commission on China. She was First Lady of Massachusetts during the governorship of her husband, William Weld.

cross_connections.display_label: First Lady of West Virginia and public broadcasting chief

bio.bio_blurb: Sinologist and First Lady of Massachusetts

notable.notable_blurb: 26th US President; Rough Rider; Nobel Peace laureate

cross_connections.display_label: his Harvard ornithology partner on an 1877 bird list
Trust-busted Morgan's Northern Securities Company in 1904
designed the townhouse his family used near the White House
drew up the guardianship papers for his children
the Columbia president and Nobel laureate he befriended

military_service.notes: Led the assault on the San Juan Heights, 1898; Medal of Honor awarded in 2001.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the childhood friend next door
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt married Edith Kermit Carow in London in 1886, his second wife and a granddaughter of the Union general Daniel Tyler. Through her he entered the Tyler family, with its Edwards and Hooker roots in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard ornithology friendship became his first publication
narrative_blocks.body: He met Henry Davis Minot, a Boston-based naturalist, as a Harvard freshman in 1877. They spent vacations listing birds in the Adirondacks and co-authored 'The Summer Birds of the Adirondacks' that fall — his first published work.

narrative_blocks.header: Trust-busted J.P. Morgan's Northern Securities Company
narrative_blocks.body: His administration brought the antitrust suit that dissolved Morgan's Northern Securities holding company in 1904, ending the merger of the Northern Pacific and Great Northern railroads. Morgan reportedly told him 'Send your man to my man and they can fix it up' — the President refused, and the Supreme Court sided with the government.

narrative_blocks.header: He befriended Columbia's president Nicholas Butler
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt's circle included Nicholas Murray Butler, the long-serving Columbia president and 1931 Nobel peace laureate, whom he nicknamed Nicholas Miraculous. The two Republican allies stayed close from Roosevelt's rise through the White House years.

narrative_blocks.header: The Roosevelts borrowed a famous architect's townhouse
narrative_blocks.body: When the White House was torn up for renovation in 1902, the family decamped to a townhouse belonging to the firm of Carrère and Hastings. Its partner Thomas Hastings would go on to design the New York Public Library and Arlington's Tomb of the Unknowns.

notable.notable_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: She built the modern First Lady's office
narrative_blocks.body: Edith hired the first salaried social secretary for a First Lady and directed the 1902 White House renovation that created the West Wing. Historians credit her with shaping the modern institution of the role.

narrative_blocks.header: She burned a lifetime of love letters
narrative_blocks.body: After Theodore died, the intensely private Edith destroyed the letters he had written her across their courtship and marriage. It is part of why her inner life stayed, by her own design, almost unknowable.

narrative_blocks.header: She wrote her own Connecticut ancestors' history
narrative_blocks.body: Late in life Edith researched her mother's Tyler family and co-wrote a history of their colonial Connecticut roots. She bought the family's ancestral home in Brooklyn, Connecticut, returning there each summer.

narrative_blocks.header: She stumped for Hoover to disown Franklin
narrative_blocks.body: Edith kept out of politics for decades, then made a rare appearance for Herbert Hoover in 1932. Part of her motive was to remind voters that the Democratic nominee, Franklin Roosevelt, was only a distant cousin and not her son.

narrative_blocks.header: Theodore regretted overruling her judgment
narrative_blocks.body: A childhood neighbor whom Theodore Roosevelt married in 1886, Edith ran Sagamore Hill and raised six children while quietly advising him on people and politics. Roosevelt admitted he was worse off whenever he ignored her counsel.

cross_connections.display_label: was her stepdaughter, the capital’s sharpest wit
her husband President Roosevelt's Hartford-born mother

bio.bio_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

career.role: First Lady of the United States

notable.notable_blurb: Merchant-philanthropist; father of President Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: He helped found three great New York institutions
narrative_blocks.body: A glass-importing merchant and tireless philanthropist, Roosevelt helped establish the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the American Museum of Natural History, and the Children's Aid Society. He poured himself into charity for the city's poor children.

narrative_blocks.header: He sold Lincoln on a soldiers' pay plan
narrative_blocks.body: Devoted to the Union though his wife's sympathies ran Confederate, Roosevelt devised an allotment system letting soldiers send pay home to their families. He lobbied it through Washington with Lincoln's help and toured the Army of the Potomac signing men up.

cross_connections.display_label: the Hooker descendant his son President Roosevelt married

bio.bio_blurb: Merchant-philanthropist; father of President Theodore Roosevelt

notable.notable_blurb: Hartford-born mother of President Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: A Georgia belle who inspired Scarlett O'Hara
narrative_blocks.body: Mittie was a celebrated Southern beauty raised at Bulloch Hall in Roswell, Georgia, said to have partly inspired Margaret Mitchell's Scarlett O'Hara. Through the Civil War she remained an unreconstructed Confederate while her husband worked for the Union.

narrative_blocks.header: Two deaths in one house on Valentine's Day
narrative_blocks.body: On Saint Valentine's Day in 1884 Mittie died of typhoid in the family home as her son Theodore kept vigil. One floor above, his first wife was dying the same day, hours after bearing his daughter.

cross_connections.display_label: the Hooker descendant her son President Roosevelt married

bio.bio_blurb: Hartford-born mother of President Theodore Roosevelt

notable.notable_blurb: Shipowner; father of First Lady Edith Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: He owned the packet ship West Point
narrative_blocks.body: Carow inherited the Kermit and Carow shipping line from Robert Kermit, his father's partner, and owned the Liverpool packet West Point through the 1860s. The Civil War's collapse of American shipping ruined the trade and his fortunes declined.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter became a president's wife
narrative_blocks.body: The Carows lived beside the Roosevelts in New York, and his daughter Edith grew up alongside young Theodore. She married him in 1886 and became First Lady fifteen years later.

bio.bio_blurb: Shipowner; father of First Lady Edith Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: From Paris polish to genteel poverty
narrative_blocks.body: Educated in Paris during her father the general’s European years, Gertrude married the shipping heir Charles Carow in 1859 — and watched his drinking dissolve the family fortune. Widowed in 1883, she eventually moved to Italy, where living was cheaper.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter grew up with the Roosevelts
narrative_blocks.body: The Carows were intimates of the East 20th Street Roosevelts, and her daughter Edith was raised in and out of Theodore’s nursery — becoming First Lady in 1901, six years after Gertrude died at Turin.

cross_connections.display_label: was the father of her husband Charles Carow
was the mother of her husband Charles Carow

bio.bio_blurb: Mother of First Lady Edith Roosevelt

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.
